Framework · free to use

Are you ready to roll out AI?

Before you sign vendor contracts or write a roadmap, run through this 25-question readiness checklist. Each "no" is either a fix-it-first item or a reason to scope smaller. None of these are technology questions — they are organization questions, which is where AI rollouts actually live or die.

By Bill Colbert · Founder, Treetop Growth Strategy
Published May 2026 · More from the library
Use this before you build the roadmap

Answer honestly

Score yourself 0 or 1 on each. Anything 0 needs attention before — or alongside — rollout. Total score of 18+: you are ready. 12-17: ready with caveats. Below 12: fix the foundations before scaling AI usage.

Strategy & leadership (5 questions)

Does the executive layer back this?

  • The CEO has stated, in writing, what AI is meant to accomplish at this company.
  • There is a named human (not a committee) responsible for the AI rollout.
  • That person has at least 8 hours per week of calendar dedicated to it.
  • The exec team agrees on the top 3 outcomes that matter — and they are NOT "adopt AI."
  • There is a budget envelope set (even if rough) for tools, training, and external help.
Operations (6 questions)

Can the company absorb new tools?

  • There is at least one repeatable workflow documented per major function (sales, marketing, ops).
  • We can name the 5 highest-leverage workflows where AI might help.
  • We have a way to measure outcomes (cycle time, output volume, hours saved) — even crude.
  • We have shipped at least one internal tool/process change in the last 12 months that the team adopted.
  • Our team understands what "in production" means — i.e., the difference between a demo and a daily-use tool.
  • We have at least one person who can write a clear prompt and refine it iteratively.
Data & security (5 questions)

Can your data go into AI tools?

  • We know what data classes exist in our company (public, internal, confidential, regulated).
  • We have written rules — even simple ones — for what data can go into external tools.
  • Our IT/security person knows what an enterprise AI platform's data handling looks like.
  • We are not currently in active remediation for a security incident.
  • If regulated (HIPAA, SOC 2, PCI, etc.), we know which AI platforms have the appropriate attestations or agreements.
Tools & access (4 questions)

Can people actually use the tools?

  • Everyone in the affected workflows can be given an enterprise AI seat without procurement gymnastics.
  • Our IT setup will not block paste, file upload, or browser extensions the tools require.
  • Our SSO supports adding new SaaS tools without a 6-week IT project.
  • We are willing to pay for enterprise AI tier, not just free tiers.
Culture & change (5 questions)

Will the team adopt this?

  • The team has been told (truthfully) that AI is meant to remove drudgery, not headcount.
  • Workflow owners are willing to change how they work — not just add a tool on the side.
  • We have a forum (Slack, regular standup, office hours) where prompts and tactics get shared.
  • We are willing to publish a one-page AI policy and ask people to follow it.
  • Senior leaders are willing to use the tools themselves — visibly — not just sponsor the work.
What to do with your score

Three readiness levels

18-25 — Ready to scale

You have the foundations. Go straight to a 90-day rollout plan with three workflows running in parallel.

12-17 — Ready with caveats

Pick one workflow, ship it well, and use the experience to build the foundations you are missing. Do not roll out everywhere yet.

Below 12 — Fix foundations first

Spend 30-60 days on the missing foundations before adding AI scope. Almost always: assign a named owner, write the data rules, and ship one small thing.
